purpose: Distilled numbers for the @clickhouse/rowbinary blog post. Generated from the existing benchmark suite (npm run bench) + skill end-to-end generation runs. Not a redesign of the eval — uses the bench files as-is.

environment.rowbinary_git_ref: main (HEAD; package @clickhouse/rowbinary 0.2.0, in-repo source — not a release tag)

note_on_ratios: hz = ops/sec from tinybench (higher = faster). Ratios are point estimates from a single bench pass; rme per benchmark ranged ~0.5%-6.5%. Ratios are robust to that noise at the reported precision.

number_1_json_vs_rowbinary_numeric.question: JSONEachRow vs the generic library-API RowBinary reader, numeric-heavy schemas, identical result sets.
number_1_json_vs_rowbinary_numeric.comparison: RowBinary API-combinator reader hz / JSONEachRow (JSON.parse) hz

iot.columns: UInt32, DateTime64(3), Float64 x3, Float32, UInt8

ledger.columns: UInt128, Int64, Decimal128(18) x2, Decimal64(4), UInt256
ledger.note: Bare JSONEachRow is 1.77x but SILENTLY WRONG: every UInt128/Int64/Decimal128/UInt256 is rounded to float64. The only correct JSON path quotes the values server-side and re-parses to bigint/decimal by hand — that is the 24.97 hz / 3.28x figure (apples-to-apples on correctness).

number_1_json_vs_rowbinary_numeric.hardware: Apple M4 Max (local). See ci_cross_check for the AMD EPYC numbers.

ci_cross_check.hardware: GitHub-hosted ubuntu-latest runner: AMD EPYC 7763 (4 vCPU), Node 24.17.0, ClickHouse 26.6.1.1193
ci_cross_check.note: CI runner is noisier (rme +-10-18%, 10-23 samples). The ledger ratio is the hardware-sensitive one: 3.3x on M4 Max, ~2.5x on the 4-core AMD cloud box. IoT is stable (~2.1x) across both.

number_1_json_vs_rowbinary_numeric.verdict: Expectation met on both hardware profiles. Cross-hardware claim: RowBinary beats a CORRECT JSON decode by ~2.5x-3.3x on numeric-heavy schemas (lower on commodity cloud CPUs, higher on Apple Silicon); iot ~2.1x is stable. The ledger figure is hardware-dependent, so cite the range, not a single number.

excluded_string_heavy_flag.columns: DateTime, LowCardinality(String) x2, String (template), hex trace_id
excluded_string_heavy_flag.finding: JSON WINS, as the skill's format-choice guidance predicts. JSONEachRow 71.69 hz and JSONCompactEachRow 91.76 hz both beat the RowBinary API reader (58.71 hz); JSONCompact even beats the optimized RowBinary reader (65.88 hz). Correctly excluded from the numeric-heavy aggregate.

number_2_monomorphic_vs_naive.question: Agent-inlined monomorphic parser vs the naive parser built by composing the library's public per-type read functions in a loop.
number_2_monomorphic_vs_naive.comparison: optimized/monomorphized reader hz / API-combinator reader hz

orders.note: win amplified by swapping the BigInt UUID formatter for the lookup-table formatUUIDTable, not pure monomorphization

non_reproducing_flag.finding: 1.5x does NOT reproduce here. A flat all-scalar row with no hot per-row formatter and no composite structure gives monomorphization nothing to remove — bounds-check coalescing alone is marginal. This is the documented 'on par, use the simpler API reader' case.

number_2_monomorphic_vs_naive.verdict: Expectation met on genuinely numeric-heavy schemas (1.55x-2.46x), holds >=1.5x across ledger/iot/orders. Flagged: events (flat scalar) is ~1.05x.

number_3_generation_cost.question: Per-parser generation cost running the skill end-to-end.
number_3_generation_cost.method: 4 isolated Claude Code subagents, each told to read SKILL.md + follow its routing and generate a monomorphized reader for one numeric-heavy schema. Token counts read from per-agent transcript usage records.
queries: (a Int64, b Int128, c UInt64, d Float64)
number_3_generation_cost.note: tokens_in is total context processed across the agent's multi-turn loop; most of it is cache re-reads of the growing transcript. The ~28k cache-write figure is the meaningful skill footprint (SKILL.md + routed reader source + repo context read once). Cost ~$0.20/parser with the harness's prompt cache; ~$0.72 if billed with no cache discount.

anecdote_uuid_failure_mode.question: Closed-book (memory only), how reliably do Sonnet 4.6 and a control frontier model write a CORRECT RowBinary UUID reader? Does the README's documented coin-flip failure mode reproduce?
anecdote_uuid_failure_mode.method: The README eval measures model MEMORY (cold recall), NOT the ability to eventually arrive at the right answer by googling/experimenting. So this is a strict closed-book test: each subagent could use ONLY the Write tool to save its function — no WebFetch, WebSearch, Bash, Read, or any verification. Transcripts confirmed all 10 samples used Write only. 5 samples per model. Each output run against live ClickHouse toUUID('61f0c404-5cb3-11e7-907b-a6006ad3dba0') FORMAT RowBinary = e711b35c04c4f061a0dbd36a00a67b90; canonical text = 61f0c404-5cb3-11e7-907b-a6006ad3dba0.

sonnet_4_6.failure_mode: hexes the 16 wire bytes in declaration order with NO half-reversal — the silently-wrong scramble. Matches the README's coin-flip claim (~3 of 5 fail).

opus_4_8_control.note: reliably recalls the two little-endian-UInt64-halves layout cold

anecdote_uuid_failure_mode.verdict: REPRODUCES. Closed-book, Sonnet 4.6 gets RowBinary UUID byte order wrong ~60% of the time (3/5), producing a silently-corrupt value; Opus 4.8 is reliable (5/5). The skill makes it correct-by-construction on every run regardless of model.

earlier_with_tools_run_CORRECTION.what_happened: An initial single A/B run gave the no-skill agents web + bash. Sonnet then WebFetched the ClickHouse docs (finding the canonical worked example) and Opus answered from memory — both produced correct output, making it look like the failure mode no longer reproduces.
earlier_with_tools_run_CORRECTION.why_it_was_wrong: That measured 'can the model eventually get there with tools', not memory. The README measures memory. With tools removed (above), the failure mode clearly reproduces on Sonnet 4.6.

with_skill_sonnet_4_6.path: read skill, correct by construction, no web lookup
